planting.note: Direct sow at 3–5 lb/acre (or ½ oz per 100 sq ft) in spring in moist to wet, heavy clay or loamy soil; inoculate seed with clover-type Rhizobium before sowing and scarify lightly if seed coat is hard.

directions: Direct sow at 3–5 lb/acre (or ½ oz per 100 sq ft) in spring in moist to wet, heavy clay or loamy soil; inoculate seed with clover-type Rhizobium before sowing and scarify lightly if seed coat is hard. Trifolium fragiferum is a low-growing, creeping perennial clover distinguished from other clovers by its unusual inflated, strawberry-like fruiting heads that give the plant its common name; it is exceptionally tolerant of waterlogging, saline soils, and heavy clay — soil conditions that defeat most other clovers — making it the best clover for coastal areas, tidal influence, and problem wet spots. As a perennial nitrogen-fixing cover crop, it establishes a persistent ground cover that can be mowed and left in place for years, fixing atmospheric nitrogen through Rhizobium root nodules. It is excellent for living mulch systems, riparian buffer strips, and orchard understories on poorly drained land.
sources: USDA SARE (Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; button medic is a winter annual in warm climates (zones 7–10) and should be sown in September–October to overwinter as a productive cover crop.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; button medic is a winter annual in warm climates (zones 7–10) and should be sown in September–October to overwinter as a productive cover crop. Medicago orbicularis is a Mediterranean annual member of the medic family named for its distinctive flat, circular, button-like seed pods — a single coil instead of the multiple-coiled pods of barrel medic (M. truncatula); it is a fast-establishing winter annual cover crop well-suited to Mediterranean climates and the mild winters of the Gulf Coast. It fixes nitrogen through Rhizobium symbiosis, produces excellent bee forage from its small yellow flowers in early spring, and dies naturally as temperatures warm, leaving a nitrogen-rich mulch that can be incorporated as green manure before spring planting; it reseeds readily in suitable conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer to fall (August–October depending on zone) at 30–60 lb/acre (or 1–2 oz per 100 sq ft) in well-drained to moderately moist, average soil in full sun; always mix with a cereal grain companion (winter rye or oats at 50–70 lb/acre) to provide trellis support, and inoculate seed with vetch-type Rhizobium before sowing.

directions: Direct sow in late summer to fall (August–October depending on zone) at 30–60 lb/acre (or 1–2 oz per 100 sq ft) in well-drained to moderately moist, average soil in full sun; always mix with a cereal grain companion (winter rye or oats at 50–70 lb/acre) to provide trellis support, and inoculate seed with vetch-type Rhizobium before sowing. Vicia villosa (hairy vetch) is the workhorse of winter cover crops, capable of fixing 80–200 lb of nitrogen per acre per season and surviving winter temperatures to -15°F (-26°C) when well established; it has a vining, hairy habit and produces spectacular spikes of small, two-toned purple-and-white pea flowers in spring that are extremely attractive to bumblebees before termination. It is one of the best cover crops for building organic matter and suppressing spring weeds; terminate by crimping or incorporation at early bloom before pods set to maximize nitrogen release and prevent volunteer seedling problems in subsequent seasons.
sources: USDA SARE Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer to early fall in average, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; incorporate into the soil as green manure when stems are in bud stage (before flowering) for best biofumigant effect — the glucosinolates in the tissues break down to isothiocyanates that suppress soil pathogens and nematodes.

directions: Direct sow in late summer to early fall in average, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; incorporate into the soil as green manure when stems are in bud stage (before flowering) for best biofumigant effect — the glucosinolates in the tissues break down to isothiocyanates that suppress soil pathogens and nematodes. Brassica juncea is a fast-growing, cold-tolerant annual Brassica crop used as both a culinary mustard (the seeds are ground for prepared mustard) and an important cover crop for biofumigation, organic weed suppression, and green manure; high-glucosinolate varieties are specifically bred for maximum biofumigant effect against root-knot nematodes, Fusarium, Verticillium, and other soil pathogens, and may reduce pathogen pressure by 30–80% compared to untreated controls. The flowers provide excellent early-spring nectar for bumblebees before spring crops are established; allow a portion of the stand to flower when using primarily for pollinator benefit rather than biofumigation.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy, neutral to slightly alkaline, low-fertility soil in full sun; scarify seed lightly and inoculate with medic-type Rhizobium before sowing; little burr medic self-seeds prolifically in suitable conditions and can maintain itself without reseeding in dryland farming systems.

directions: Direct sow in fall in dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy, neutral to slightly alkaline, low-fertility soil in full sun; scarify seed lightly and inoculate with medic-type Rhizobium before sowing; little burr medic self-seeds prolifically in suitable conditions and can maintain itself without reseeding in dryland farming systems. Medicago minima is a prostrate, hairy annual medic native to dry, disturbed, sandy soils of the Mediterranean region and widely naturalized throughout the dryland regions of the world; it produces tiny, yellow, pea-like flowers and distinctive spiny, coiled seed pods with hooked spines (burrs) that aid in seed dispersal, and fixes nitrogen through Rhizobium root nodules in poor, thin, sandy soils where clover and alfalfa struggle. It is particularly useful as a self-reseeding annual cover crop in dryland farming systems, sandy coastal gardens, and dry herb garden paths where its low, prostrate habit and nitrogen-fixing ability improve soil fertility with minimal water input.
